cards.back: A capture allows a lambda to access variables from the enclosing scope. Captured variables are specified in the square brackets [].

cards.text: To capture a variable by value, use {{c1::[varName]}}. To capture by reference, use {{c2::[&varName]}}.

cards.question: What does this lambda print?
cards.answer:
Prints: 5
Lambda captured x by value, so it has a copy of the original value (5), not affected by later changes.
cards.explanation: Capture by value [x] makes a copy when the lambda is created. Changes to x afterward don't affect the captured copy.

cards.question: What does this lambda print?
cards.answer:
Prints: 10
Lambda captured x by reference, so it sees the current value of x when called.
cards.explanation: Capture by reference [&x] creates a reference to the original variable. Changes to x are visible to the lambda.

cards.text: Default capture by value: {{c1::[=]}}. Default capture by reference: {{c2::[&]}}. These capture {{c3::all}} variables used in the lambda.

cards.question: What do these capture clauses mean?
cards.answer:
[=, &y]: Capture all by value, except y by reference
[&, x]: Capture all by reference, except x by value
[x, &y]: Capture x by value and y by reference (no default)
cards.explanation: Can mix default captures with specific captures. Default must come first, then specific captures override it.

cards.front: What's the danger of capturing local variables by reference?
cards.back: If the lambda outlives the captured variable (e.g., lambda returned from function), the reference becomes dangling, leading to undefined behavior when accessed.

cards.text: Variables captured by value are {{c1::const}} by default. To modify them, use the {{c2::mutable}} keyword: [x]() mutable { x++; }

cards.question: What's the difference between these lambdas?
cards.code:
int x = 5;
auto f1 = [x]() { x++; }; // Error!
auto f2 = [x]() mutable { x++; }; // OK
cards.answer:
f1: Compile error - can't modify captured value without mutable
f2: OK - mutable allows modifying the captured copy (original x unchanged)
cards.explanation: By default, value captures are const. mutable keyword makes them modifiable (but still doesn't affect the original).

cards.front: Should you prefer capture by value or capture by reference?
cards.back:
Prefer capture by value [=] for safety (no dangling references). Use capture by reference [&] only when:
  • Need to modify the original variable
  • Copying is expensive
  • Lambda won't outlive the variable

cards.text: Empty capture clause {{c1::[]}} means the lambda is {{c2::stateless}} and captures nothing from the enclosing scope.

cards.question: How do you capture the this pointer in a lambda?
cards.code:
class MyClass {
    int value = 42;
    void doWork() {
        auto lambda = [this]() { std::cout << value; };
    }
};
cards.answer:
[this] captures the this pointer, allowing access to member variables and functions.
In C++17+, can use [*this] to capture by value (copy entire object).
cards.explanation: Capturing this gives access to class members. [this] is by pointer, [*this] is by value (C++17).

Follow these guidelines:
  • Avoid default captures [=] and [&] - be explicit about what you capture
  • Prefer capture by value for safety
  • Use capture by reference only for local, short-lived lambdas
  • Never return a lambda that captures local variables by reference
  • Capture only what you need, not everything

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Dangling reference! Lambda captures x by reference, but x is destroyed when makeLambda() returns. Calling the lambda causes undefined behavior.
cards.explanation: Never return a lambda that captures local variables by reference. Use capture by value instead: [x]

cards.text: C++14 introduced {{c1::init captures}} (generalized lambda captures): [newVar = expression] to {{c2::create new variables}} in the capture clause.

cards.question: What does this C++14 init capture do?
cards.code:
int x = 5;
auto lambda = [y = x * 2]() { return y; };
std::cout << lambda();
cards.answer:
Prints: 10
Init capture [y = x * 2] creates a new variable y initialized to x * 2 (10) inside the lambda.
cards.explanation: Init captures let you transform or rename captured values. y exists only in lambda scope.

cards.front: How do you move a variable into a lambda capture (C++14)?
cards.back:
Use init capture with std::move:
[ptr = std::move(uniquePtr)]() { /* use ptr */ }
This transfers ownership into the lambda.

cards.question: What happens when you capture a const variable?
cards.code:
const int x = 10;
auto f1 = [x]() { return x * 2; };
auto f2 = [&x]() { return x * 2; };
cards.answer: Both work fine. f1 captures const value by value, f2 by reference. Both can read x but neither can modify it (it's const).
cards.explanation: Capturing const variables is safe either way. The const-ness is preserved in the lambda.
